policy_name: Culture of Safety and Quality Policy
purpose: This policy describes how <<LAB_NAME>> builds and sustains a culture in which staff feel safe to report errors, near-misses, and quality concerns without fear of blame. CLIA at 42 CFR 493.1232 requires confidentiality and integrity of patient data; the culture-of-safety expectation is implicit in the quality system requirements at 42 CFR 493.1200.

cfr_text_blocks.label: Condition: Quality systems
cfr_text_blocks.verbatim: Each laboratory that performs nonwaived testing must establish and maintain written policies and procedures that implement and monitor a quality system for all phases of the total testing process (that is, preanalytic, analytic, and postanalytic) as well as general laboratory systems.

scope: All <<LAB_NAME>> personnel, leadership, and the medical director or designee.
policy_statements: <<LAB_NAME>> commits to a non-punitive approach to error and near-miss reporting: staff who report in good faith are not subject to discipline solely for the report.

Errors caused by reckless behavior, willful misconduct, or knowing violation of policy are subject to disciplinary action; honest mistakes and system failures are addressed through process improvement, not blame.

Every staff member is trained at hire and annually on the lab's error reporting channels, non-retaliation commitment, and the distinction between system failure and individual misconduct (the 'Just Culture' framework).

Quality and safety metrics (error rate, near-miss reports, complaint volume, staff-perceived safety climate) are tracked and reviewed at least annually by the medical director or designee; trends drive system change.

Leadership models culture-of-safety behaviors: visible response to reports, acknowledgment of contributions, transparency about findings and actions, and consistent application of the just-culture distinctions.
procedure_steps: On hire, every staff member receives culture-of-safety training including the non-punitive reporting commitment and the Just Culture framework; refreshed annually.
Errors and near-misses are reported on the Quality Event log; reports are acknowledged within 5 business days.
Investigation distinguishes between system failure, at-risk behavior (lapse), and reckless behavior; corrective action is matched to the category.
Annual culture-of-safety review by the medical director or designee assesses metrics and survey data; findings drive system change.
definitions: Framework distinguishing human error (managed by system change), at-risk behavior (managed by coaching), and reckless behavior (managed by discipline).
The lab's commitment that staff reporting errors or near-misses in good faith are not subject to discipline solely for the report.
